teamObjective.explanation: With only low non-trump clubs, leading the smallest card gives partner the best chance to win the trick and take control, while I preserve my slightly higher cards for later.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) holds 3 unknown cards. With no public play yet, partner is likely to have some mid/high non-trump or trump cards that can beat a low club lead, but this is uncertain.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponent player 1 has only 2 cards, suggesting they may be close to finishing, but hand count alone does not reveal strength. Opponents likely have cards that can beat a 3 of clubs.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of clubs is the lowest-risk opening: it expends the weakest card, keeps the 4, 5, and 6 for future plays, and maximizes the chance that partner can overtake with a higher single or trump.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Only marginally stronger than the 3 and still very weak; playing it now wastes a slightly more useful card without significantly improving the chance of winning the trick.
The 5 is one of my better cards and could be useful later; leading it now provides little extra protection against opponents or partner support.
Although the highest card in hand, a 6 of clubs is still low and likely beaten by opponents or partner; keeping it for a later trick is better resource management.

risks: An opponent may play a higher single or trump and win the lead.
Partner may not be able to beat the low lead, ceding control to opponents.
Player 1 is near finishing and could capitalize on the lead.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the absolute lowest card minimizes my own commitment and leaves my remaining cards available to respond later; partner still has a chance to capture the trick.

notes: No public play has occurred, so all beliefs about partner and opponent hands remain probabilistic.
